county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Franklin County formed 1867 from Kearney County. County seat is Franklin. South-central Nebraska along the Republican River valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Franklin County (formed 1867, seat Franklin) in south-central Nebraska was created from Kearney County. Records are at the courthouse in Franklin. The Republican River valley runs through the county. Homestead entries dominate early land records -- check BLM GLO records. Settlement accelerated in the 1870s-1880s. German, Swedish, and Anglo-American families were the primary settlers. The Franklin County Historical Society may have local materials. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Franklin County's agricultural heritage means family farms and their associated records (chattel mortgages, farm implements) appear in county recorder files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Franklin County. County Register of Deeds in Franklin holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Franklin County Register of Deeds, Franklin
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1867 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1867. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Probate and Estate Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1867. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.source_name: Franklin County Federal and State Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Franklin County (1870-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Frontier County formed 1872 from unorganized territory. County seat is Stockville. Southwestern Nebraska. Parent to Gosper County (1873). Ranching and farming.
county_metadata.research_tips: Frontier County (formed 1872, seat Stockville) in southwestern Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Stockville. Frontier County was settled by homesteaders in the 1870s-1880s. The Medicine Creek area drew early cattle ranchers. BLM GLO records document homestead and Timber Culture Act entries. Eustis, Curtis, and Stockville were the main communities. German and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The 1885 state census is available. Frontier County was parent to Gosper County (1873) -- earlier records may be shared. The Curtis Enterprise and other local newspapers are useful sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Frontier County. County Register of Deeds in Stockville holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Furnas County formed 1873 from unorganized territory. County seat is Beaver City. South-central Nebraska along the Republican River. Arapahoe and Cambridge are significant communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: Furnas County (formed 1873, seat Beaver City) in south-central Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Beaver City. The Republican River runs through the county. Settlement came through homesteading in the 1870s-1880s. BLM GLO records document homestead entries. Beaver City, Arapahoe, and Cambridge were the main communities. German, Scandinavian, and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Furnas County Historical Society may have local materials. Cambridge's newspaper history provides local genealogical information.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Furnas County. County Register of Deeds in Beaver City holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Gage County formed 1855 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Beatrice. Site of the first homestead claim under the Homestead Act of 1862 (Daniel Freeman, January 1, 1863). Homestead National Historical Park.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gage County (formed 1855, seat Beatrice) is one of the original Nebraska Territory counties and historically significant as the site of the first homestead claim under the Homestead Act of 1862 (Daniel Freeman, January 1, 1863). The Homestead National Historical Park in Beatrice preserves this history. Records are at the courthouse in Beatrice. The Gage County Historical Society Museum has extensive local collections. Being among the earliest counties, Gage has territorial-era records. The Big Blue River valley attracted settlers from the 1850s. German, Czech/Bohemian, and Anglo-American settlers were numerous. Burlington and Missouri River Railroad land sales are documented. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of deed, probate, marriage, and court records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska holds important territorial-era manuscripts. Beatrice newspapers from the 1860s are valuable sources.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Gage County. County Register of Deeds in Beatrice holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.repository: History Nebraska / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Gage County communities. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage announcements, homestead proof notices, estate settlements, and community news. History Nebraska holds extensive microfilm of Nebraska newspapers. Chronicling America has digitized selections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Garden County formed 1909 from Deuel County. County seat is Oshkosh. One of Nebraska's youngest counties. Panhandle county. Ash Hollow (Oregon Trail landmark). Kinkaid Act homesteads.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garden County (formed 1909, seat Oshkosh) is one of Nebraska's youngest counties, carved from Deuel County. Records are at the courthouse in Oshkosh. This western Panhandle county was settled primarily through homesteading -- the Kinkaid Act (1904) was important for this area. BLM GLO records document land entries. Ash Hollow, a famous Oregon Trail landmark, is in Garden County. Cattle ranching and dry-land farming are the economic base. The county's late formation means pre-1909 records are in Deuel County. FamilySearch has limited coverage. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The small population means limited genealogical infrastructure, but land records at the Register of Deeds are well-preserved.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Garden County. County Register of Deeds in Oshkosh holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Kinkaid Act Homestead Records -- Garden County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kinkaid Act (1904) homestead records for Garden County. The Kinkaid Act allowed 640-acre homestead claims in the Sandhills and other western Nebraska counties, recognizing that 160 acres was insufficient for ranching in semi-arid grasslands. Original case files at NARA; patents at BLM GLO.

source_profiles.source_name: Overland Trail Records -- Garden County Area
source_profiles.repository: History Nebraska / NARA / Oregon-California Trails Association
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Trail-era records for the Garden County area along the Oregon, California, and/or Mormon Trails. Emigrant diaries, station records, and military escort reports. History Nebraska holds manuscript collections. Most trail traffic passed through before county organization.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Garfield County formed 1884 from Wheeler County. County seat is Burwell. North-central Nebraska. North Loup River valley. Known for its rodeo tradition.
county_metadata.research_tips: Garfield County (formed 1884, seat Burwell) in north-central Nebraska was created from Wheeler County. Records are at the courthouse in Burwell. Burwell is known for its rodeo tradition. Homestead entries dominate early land records -- check BLM GLO records. The North Loup River valley influenced settlement. Settlement came in the 1880s primarily from eastern states. The Garfield County Historical Society may have local materials. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. The 1885 state census may have early enumeration. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Seventh-Day Adventist settlers in the North Loup Valley are documented in church records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Garfield County. County Register of Deeds in Burwell holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Gosper County formed 1873 from unorganized territory near Frontier County. County seat is Elwood. Small south-central Nebraska county. Platte River's south channel area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Gosper County (formed 1873, seat Elwood) in south-central Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory near Frontier County. Records are at the courthouse in Elwood. This small county was settled by homesteaders in the 1870s-1880s. BLM GLO records document homestead entries. The Platte River's south channel borders the county. Johnson Lake (modern reservoir) covers some former farmland. German and Anglo-American settlers predominated. FamilySearch has limited microfilm. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. Researchers should check neighboring Dawson, Frontier, and Phelps county records for related families given the small population.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Gosper County. County Register of Deeds in Elwood holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Grant County formed 1887 from unorganized territory. County seat is Hyannis. Sandhills county. One of Nebraska's smallest populations. Cattle ranching. Kinkaid Act homesteads.
county_metadata.research_tips: Grant County (formed 1887, seat Hyannis) is a Sandhills county with one of Nebraska's smallest populations. Records are at the courthouse in Hyannis. The Kinkaid Act (1904) drove permanent settlement, allowing 640-acre homesteads in this sandy grassland. BLM GLO records are essential for land entries. Cattle ranching is virtually the only industry. The sparse population means very limited genealogical infrastructure. FamilySearch has minimal coverage. History Nebraska holds what supplementary materials exist. Federal censuses from 1900 onward are the primary population records. Researchers should check neighboring Arthur, Hooker, and Keith county records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Grant County. County Register of Deeds in Hyannis holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: History Nebraska (NSHS) holds microfilm copies of Grant County records including deed indexes, probate records, marriage records, county board proceedings, and selected court records. Also holds manuscript collections, photographs, and maps related to Grant County history.

source_profiles.source_name: Kinkaid Act Homestead Records -- Grant County
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Kinkaid Act (1904) homestead records for Grant County. The Kinkaid Act allowed 640-acre homestead claims in the Sandhills and other western Nebraska counties, recognizing that 160 acres was insufficient for ranching in semi-arid grasslands. Original case files at NARA; patents at BLM GLO.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Greeley County formed 1871 from unorganized territory. County seat is Greeley. Central Nebraska. Notable Irish Catholic colony (Bishop O'Connor's colony). North Loup River valley.
county_metadata.research_tips: Greeley County (formed 1871, seat Greeley) in central Nebraska was carved from unorganized territory. Records are at the courthouse in Greeley. The county is notable for strong Irish Catholic settlement -- the Irish Colony brought families to the Greeley area in the 1870s under the sponsorship of Bishop James O'Connor. Catholic parish records are essential (Sacred Heart, Greeley; and other parishes). Homestead entries at BLM GLO document original settlers. The North Loup River valley influenced settlement. FamilySearch has microfilmed county records. The 1885 state census is available. History Nebraska holds supplementary materials. The Greeley County Historical Society may have local materials. Irish heritage records may connect to County Cork, County Clare, and other Irish origins.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Greeley County. County Register of Deeds in Greeley holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Greeley County Register of Deeds, Greeley
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1871 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Marriage Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1871. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Probate and Estate Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1871. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Federal and State Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Greeley County (1880-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1890 federal census destroyed nationally; Nebraska 1885 state census survives

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Federal Land Patents -- BLM GLO
source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Greeley County including homestead entries, pre-emption claims, timber culture entries, cash sales, and military warrant locations. Nebraska is a public land state -- all original land transfers from federal ownership are documented here. Searchable online by name and location.

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Historical Society
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Greeley County Historical Society / Nebraska State Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions and burial records for Greeley County. Find A Grave has growing coverage. Township, church, and family cemeteries are common in rural Nebraska. County historical societies and the Nebraska State Genealogical Society have published cemetery transcription volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Records on Microfilm -- History Nebraska
source_profiles.coverage_notes: History Nebraska (NSHS) holds microfilm copies of Greeley County records including deed indexes, probate records, marriage records, county board proceedings, and selected court records. Also holds manuscript collections, photographs, and maps related to Greeley County history.

source_profiles.source_name: Greeley County Irish Catholic Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Omaha / Diocese of Grand Island / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Irish Catholic parish records for Greeley County congregations. Baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials. Nebraska's Irish colonies (O'Neill, Greeley, Albion areas) produced rich parish records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Hall County formed 1858 as original Nebraska Territory county. County seat is Grand Island. Major central Nebraska county. Union Pacific Railroad division point. Strong German and Danish settlement.
county_metadata.research_tips: Hall County (formed 1858, seat Grand Island) is a major central Nebraska county. Records are at the courthouse in Grand Island. The Stuhr Museum of the Prairie Pioneer in Grand Island has extensive regional history and genealogical collections including family files, photographs, and manuscript collections. Grand Island was a Union Pacific Railroad division point and a major settlement destination. German immigrants (particularly from Schleswig-Holstein and Hanover) were the dominant ethnic group. Danish settlers were also significant. Homestead entries at BLM GLO are key land records. FamilySearch has extensive microfilm of Hall County records. The territorial and 1885 state censuses are available. History Nebraska holds supplementary records. Grand Island newspapers from the 1860s are valuable sources. The Grand Island Public Library has city directories and local history collections.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO patents for original federal land entries in Hall County. County Register of Deeds in Grand Island holds subsequent land transfers. Many counties do not have online access to county-level land records.

source_profiles.source_name: Hall County Deed Records -- Register of Deeds
source_profiles.repository: Hall County Register of Deeds, Grand Island
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1858 to present. Nebraska is a public land state; initial federal patents are at BLM GLO (glorecords.blm.gov). Subsequent transfers recorded at the county Register of Deeds. Includes deeds, mortgages, liens, and plats.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records intact from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage records from 1858. Nebraska marriage licenses issued by county judges (County Court). State registration began 1909. Earlier marriages recorded at county level. FamilySearch has indexed many Nebraska marriages.

source_profiles.source_name: Hall County Probate and Estate Records -- County Court
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1858. Nebraska probate is handled by the County Court. Records include wills, letters of administration, estate inventories, guardianship files, and adoption records.

source_profiles.source_name: Hall County Federal and State Census Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census records for Hall County (1860-1950). The 1890 census was destroyed by fire. Nebraska conducted a state census in 1885 (available on microfilm). Territorial censuses (1854-1856, 1860) exist for early counties. Available through FamilySearch and Ancestry.
source_profiles.survival_notes: 1890 federal census destroyed nationally; Nebraska 1885 state census survives

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Hall County including homestead entries, pre-emption claims, timber culture entries, cash sales, and military warrant locations. Nebraska is a public land state -- all original land transfers from federal ownership are documented here. Searchable online by name and location.

source_profiles.source_name: Hall County Cemetery Records -- Find A Grave / Historical Society
source_profiles.repository: Find A Grave / Hall County Historical Society / Nebraska State Genealogical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Cemetery transcriptions and burial records for Hall County. Find A Grave has growing coverage. Township, church, and family cemeteries are common in rural Nebraska. County historical societies and the Nebraska State Genealogical Society have published cemetery transcription volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Hall County Records on Microfilm -- History Nebraska
source_profiles.coverage_notes: History Nebraska (NSHS) holds microfilm copies of Hall County records including deed indexes, probate records, marriage records, county board proceedings, and selected court records. Also holds manuscript collections, photographs, and maps related to Hall County history.

source_profiles.source_name: Hall County German Lutheran and Catholic Church Records
source_profiles.repository: Archdiocese of Omaha / Diocese of Lincoln / Diocese of Grand Island / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: German Lutheran and Catholic church records for Hall County congregations. Baptisms, confirmations, marriages, and burials. Records often in German. German-Russian (Volga German) congregations may have separate records.

source_profiles.source_name: Hall County Historic Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: History Nebraska / Library of Congress / Newspapers.com
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historic newspapers for Hall County communities. Obituaries, legal notices, marriage announcements, homestead proof notices, estate settlements, and community news. History Nebraska holds extensive microfilm of Nebraska newspapers. Chronicling America has digitized selections.

source_profiles.source_name: Union Pacific Railroad Land Records -- Hall County
source_profiles.repository: Union Pacific Historical Collection / History Nebraska / Nebraska State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Union Pacific Railroad land grant and sale records for Hall County. The UP received alternate sections of land along its right-of-way. Land department records document sales to settlers, town lot sales, and colony arrangements. UP Historical Collection at History Nebraska.
